skill.name: bun-development
skill.description: Guidance for building, testing, bundling, and migrating JavaScript and TypeScript projects with the Bun runtime.
skill.summary: Develop JavaScript and TypeScript projects with Bun package management, testing, bundling, and migration guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The skill is documentation-only and I found no prompt-injection text or confirmed malicious intent. However, high-risk copy-paste examples are present, including remote install scripts piped to shells, PowerShell iex execution, third-party registries, env access, filesystem reads, and package execution commands.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Scripts Executed Through Shells
high_findings.description: The installation and migration sections instruct users to run remote installer content through bash or PowerShell iex. This is a true positive because copied commands can execute network-delivered code before users inspect it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell and iex patterns are present in installation guidance. The content appears legitimate for Bun setup, but the execution pattern is inherently high risk for marketplace users.

high_findings.title: Non-Standard Package Registry Example
high_findings.description: The package management section demonstrates installing from https://registry.npmmirror.com. This is not malicious by itself, but it can route dependency resolution through a third-party registry with different trust and availability properties.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The registry URL is explicitly shown in a package install command. The example has a legitimate purpose, but users need clear review before changing package trust sources.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many shell command examples. Most are normal Bun workflows, but they still cause local project changes, install packages, remove dependency folders, run package binaries, or build executables when copied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples rather than hidden automation. They are still operational commands with local side effects, so the external-command risk is real but contextual.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variable and Env File Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill explains automatic .env loading and shows API key and database URL access. This is legitimate Bun guidance, but it touches secrets and should warn users not to expose or log sensitive values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly reference API keys, database URLs, env files, and process environment values. There is no exfiltration evidence, so the concern is secret-handling exposure rather than malicious access.

low_findings.title: Network API Examples Are Local Server Handlers
low_findings.description: Fetch detections are examples of Bun.serve request handlers and local server output, not outbound data exfiltration. Hardcoded resource links point to Bun, GitHub, Elysia, and Bun Discord documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch identifiers are server callback names in documentation snippets. I found no evidence that the skill sends secrets or project data to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and SQLite Examples Are Instructional
low_findings.description: The filesystem and SQLite detections show basic local file reads and a local database filename. These are standard Bun examples and do not show hidden reads of sensitive paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use relative demonstration paths such as mydb.sqlite and ./data.txt. No evidence found of credential harvesting or traversal logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography static hits do not correspond to weak hashing algorithms in context. The password example uses Bun.password.hash with bcrypt options, while other hits are test framework text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted review found password hashing examples around the security-relevant section, not MD5 or SHA-1 usage. The cited test lines import and describe Bun test cases.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: I reviewed the skill text for override language, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip analysis. No evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file begins with normal skill metadata and a Bun documentation introduction. I did not find suspicious instruction-hijacking language in the inspected skill content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installer Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote installer content is piped directly to bash and PowerShell iex. Marketplace users should verify installer contents or use package-manager installation before running these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous command form is explicit and repeated. It is a common legitimate install shortcut, but it materially increases execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Execution and Registry Trust Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that execute package binaries and change package registry sources. These patterns can be safe in known projects but should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are normal developer workflows, but they delegate execution or package resolution to external ecosystems. No malicious endpoint was found.

content.value_statement: Bun changes common JavaScript workflows across installs, testing, servers, and builds. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for practical Bun development.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to initialize Bun projects and configure package scripts.
Shows Bun package management commands for installing, updating, and removing dependencies.
Covers Bun runtime execution, watch mode, hot reload, and environment variable access.
Provides examples for Bun.serve, WebSocket handling, SQLite usage, and password hashing.
Documents Bun test runner patterns, mocking, bundling, compilation, and Node.js migration steps.
Includes quick reference commands and links to official Bun and related resources.
limitations: Some install examples execute remote scripts and require manual trust review.
The guidance may lag behind current Bun releases and should be checked against official documentation.
It provides examples, not automated project analysis or migration execution.
Framework compatibility details are limited to common Bun and Node.js patterns.

use_cases.title: Start a Bun Service
use_cases.description: Create a TypeScript service with Bun.serve, package scripts, tests, and a production build workflow.

use_cases.title: Migrate From Node.js
use_cases.description: Compare Node.js patterns with Bun equivalents and plan safe changes to scripts, dependencies, and runtime APIs.

use_cases.title: Improve JavaScript Tooling Speed
use_cases.description: Evaluate Bun package installs, testing, bundling, and executable builds for faster local development.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Bun Project Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bun-development skill to plan a new TypeScript project with Bun. Include setup steps, scripts, testing, and build guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Node Scripts to Bun
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bun-development skill to review my current Node.js package scripts and suggest Bun equivalents with compatibility notes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Bun API Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bun-development skill to outline a Bun.serve API with routes, environment configuration, tests, and production bundling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Bun Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bun-development skill to review a proposed Node.js to Bun migration for runtime differences, dependency risks, and test coverage gaps.

output_examples.input: Plan a Bun project for a small TypeScript API.
output_examples.output: A setup checklist covering project initialization, dependency choices, server structure, environment handling, tests, and production build steps.

output_examples.input: Help migrate my Node.js service to Bun.
output_examples.output: A migration review that separates compatible Node.js APIs, Bun-specific replacements, package script changes, and areas needing manual testing.

output_examples.input: Explain how to use Bun for testing and bundling.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow describing Bun test patterns, watch mode, coverage, bundling targets, minification, and release checks.

best_practices: Verify remote installer scripts before running them, especially commands that pipe network content into a shell.
Keep secrets in local environment files and avoid logging API keys or database URLs.
Test Node.js compatibility before replacing existing package scripts with Bun commands.
anti_patterns: Do not copy install commands from documentation without reviewing the source and trust boundary.
Do not switch package registries in shared projects without team approval.
Do not assume every Node.js library behaves identically under Bun.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps with Bun project setup, package management, runtime APIs, testing, bundling, and Node.js migration planning.

faq.question: Can it automatically migrate a project?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and review prompts, but users must apply and test changes in their repository.

faq.question: Is it safe to run every command shown?
faq.answer: No. Review commands first, especially remote install scripts, package execution commands, and registry changes.

faq.question: Does it cover TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Bun-friendly TypeScript setup, direct TypeScript execution, build examples, and test patterns.

faq.question: Does it replace official Bun documentation?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes common workflows and should be checked against official Bun documentation for current behavior.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
